docs.title: Project Overview
docs.text:
This project focuses on analyzing and redesigning the Magic Stronghold website using UX and UI design principles. The goal is to improve usability, accessibility, visual hierarchy, and the overall user experience, while documenting the process.

The original website is an online storefront and event hub for trading card games, tournaments, and hobby products. However, the current design presents several usability challenges that affect how users navigate and interact with the site.

docs.title: Problems
docs.text:
The existing website has many issues that go against core UX/UI principles and Usability Heuristics.

  • Outdated visual design that lacks consistency. View
  • Unclear navigation structure, making it more difficult to find key information. View
  • Poor visual hierarchy, where important elements are not highlighted. View
  • Cluttered layout that reduces readability and usability. View

These problems make it harder for users to look for products, find events, and engage with the website effectively.

docs.title: Project Goals
docs.text:
The redesign is focused on creating a more user-friendly exerience by:

  • Simplifying and improving navigation.
  • Creating consistent layouts across pages.
  • Enhancing visual hierarchy and readability.
  • Applying strong UX/UI design fundamentals.
  • Improving accessibility for different users.
  • Keeping in mind good usability heuristics.

docs.title: Team
docs.text: This project was completed in a team of three.

docs.title: Project Summary
docs.text:
This project focused on redesigning the Magic Stronghold Website by applying UX and UI design principles throughout multiple points, including research, problem identification, user analysis, design exploration, testing, and refinement.

By following a structured UX process, the team was able to identify key usability issues and develop a more intuitive, consistent, and user-friendly redesign.

docs.title: What We Learned
docs.text:
Throughout this project, several important concepts were learned:

  • The importance of user-centered design when making design decisions
  • How to apply UX methodology to guide the design process
  • The value of personas, user stories, and user flows in understanding user behavior
  • The impact of usability heuristics on improving user experience
  • How user testing reveals issues that are not always visible during design
  • The role of visual design elements such as color, typography, spacing, and alignment

These concepts helped make sure that the redesign was not based on assumptions, but on structured reasoning and user needs which was made possible from personas, user scenarios and user stories.

docs.title: Design Improvements
docs.text:
Compared to the original website, the redesigned version shows clear improvements in these aspects:

  • More consistent layout and navigation
  • Improved visual hierarchy and readability
  • Better organization of products and events
  • Clearer user flows for key actions
  • Improved feedback through confirmation messages
  • More polished and modern visual design

These improvements contribute to a more intuitive and efficient user experience.

docs.title: Final Thoughts
docs.text:
This project showed that UX/UI design is not just about making a website look better, but about making it easier and more enjoyable to use. Throughout the process, we learned that small details like spacing, alignment, and user feedback can make a big difference in how users experience a site.

User testing was one of the most valuable parts of the project, as it revealed issues that were not obvious during design. It highlighted the importance of iteration and being open to feedback.

Overall, this project was a strong learning experience that helped build a better understanding of how thoughtful design decisions can improve both usability and user satisfaction.

docs.title: Comms / UX / UI Design Redesign Project - Class 1
Project Overview
docs.text:
This project focuses on analyzing and redesigning an existing website using UX and UI design principles, while documenting the full design process.

The main goal is to improve: - Usability - Accessibility - Visual hierarchy - Overall user experience - Clarity of the website's purpose

docs.title: Website Options
docs.text:
As a team, we evaluated several websites for potential redesign:

  • Magic Stronghold
  • Craigslist Vancouver
  • 4chan

Each option was considered on its usability, complexity, and potential for improvement.

docs.title: Reason for Selection
docs.text:
Magic Stronghold was selected because the current website shows several usability and design problems that go against core principles of UX and UI design. The interface appears visually outdated, navigation structure is not clear. In addition, the visual hierarchy is all over the place, and key UX/UI design principles are not applied.

In addition, the purpose of the website is not clearly communicated. Users may be unsure whether the main focus is purchasing products or participating in events, which also affects how they interact with the web site.

docs.title: Redesign Goals
docs.text: The redeisgn will focus on simplifying navigation, improving layout consistency, enhancing overall readability, and visual hierarchy. Our team will also apply recognized UX heuristics and modern visual design practices to create a more intuitive and complete user experience.

docs.title: Target Audience
docs.text:
The primary audience includes users interested in trading card games, board games, and hobby-related products, as well as those who participate in community events such as tournaments.

The redesign will consider both new users, who require clear guidance and structure, and returning users, who expect fast and efficient access to information.

docs.title: UX / UI Principles Applied
docs.text:
The initial analysis was guided by key UX/UI principles such as consistency and standards, visibility of system status, user control and freedom, and aesthetic and minimalist design. Accessibility was also considered to ensure the website can be used by a wider range of users.

These principles can help identify usability issues and will continue to guide future design decisions.

docs.title: Tools Used
docs.text:
• Figma
  • VS Code
  • Markdown

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
This week focused on identifying usability issues and defining the direction of the redesign.

This early analysis will provide a strong start for the future of the project, including personas, design exploration, and iterative improvements.

docs.title: Comms / UX / UI Design Redesign Project - Class 2
docs.text: This week was to explore the stages of UX methodology and discuss key usability factors that can influence design decisions.

docs.title: Five stages of UX Methodlogy
docs.text:
UX methodology provides an understanding to the user and helps develop effective design strategies. Having a clear process helps developers create a more user-focused application.

The first stage is Emphathize, where we seek to understand users by identifying their needs, frustration, and goals. This is done through creating user stories, observations, and problem statements.

The next stage is Define, where we analyze the core problems we have discovered. This is done by having a problem statement focusing on the issues and solving it.

The next stage is Ideate, this stage focuses on brainstorming and creating as many possible solutions as possible. The goal here is to be creative and explore all possible approaches.

The next stage is Prototype, this is where we take the most promising solutions and we test them. This gives an idea to the team on how a solution might function in a live environment.

The final stage is Test, which involves releasing prototypes to users and getting feedback and to identify areas they can improve.

docs.title: 7 Usability Factors
docs.text:
The seven usability factors provides a checklist for evaluating how well a product or service meets a users needs. Together, this ensures that the design is practical and visually appealing.

  • Useful — Is the product or service useful?
  • Usable — Is the product or service easy to use?
  • Findable — Can users easily locate what they need?
  • Credible — Does the product inspire trust?
  • Desirable — Is the product visually and emotionally appealing?
  • Accessible — Can people of varying abilities use it?
  • Valuable — Does it deliver value to users and the business?

docs.title: In-Class Real-World UX/UI Example: Ketchup Bottle Redesign
docs.text:
A strong example we disussed in class the the UX design of a traditional glass ketchup bottle. The older glass bottles were poorly designed because users often had difficulties getting the ketchup out of the bottle cleanly. This created a frustrating experience.

Over time, designers redesigned the product into a plastic squeeze bottle that also had the cap at the bottom. This redesign improved user experience greatly by allowing better control.

This example showed how even small UX design can make a simple everyday product many times better.

docs.title: Applying the UX Methodology
docs.text:
After analyzing the ketchup bottle, the design process can be linked to the UX methodology:

Empathize — Users experienced frustration with glass bottles.

Define — The product was messy, inconsistent, and required effort.

Ideate — Designers explored new materials and dispensing options.

Prototype — The plastic squeeze bottle design was developed.

Test — Users quickly confirmed the new design was faster, cleaner, easier, and just better.

docs.title: Usability Factors
docs.text:
Useful — Dispenses ketchup efficiently.

Usable — Requires less effort and strength.

Findable — Opening cap is easy to locate.

Credible — Trusted and reliable function.

Desirable — More modern and convenient.

Accessible — Easier for children and users with limited strength.

Valuable — Reduces waste and improves satisfaction.

docs.title: UX in Everyday Products
docs.text:
UX design is not just limited to websites and applications, it also works for everyday products like ketchup bottles and remote controls.

Well designed products feel intuitive and require little effort from users. In contrast, poorly designed products often cause frustration.

docs.title: The Relationship Between UX/UI and Business Success
docs.text:
Effective UX/UI design has a direct impact on business performance. Products are designed well and easy to use have higher user satisfaction, which lead to higher user retention.

While poor UX/UI design can decrease revenue, lower user retention, and negative branding.

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
This week showed that effective UX design follows a structured process rather than guessing. The UX principles and usability factors provide a clear way for understanding user needs and guiding design decisions.

Applying this process makes sure that designs are not only visually appealing, but also functional, user-focused, and effective.

docs.title: Comms / UX / UI Design Redesign Project - Class 3
docs.text:
This week focused on applying user-centered design principles to the website redesign. The goal was to design based on real user needs rather than personal assumptions.

This approach reinforces the importance of UX in creating products that are both functional and aligned with business goals.

docs.title: Topics
docs.text:
• Personas
  • User Scenarios
  • User Stories
  • UX Artifacts

These concepts focus on understanding users and translating their needs into meaningful design decisions. Instead of assuming user behavior, these tools help create realistic scenarios that guide the redesign process.

docs.title: What we did
docs.text:
During class, the team created multiple personas to represent different types of users who may visit the Magic Stronghold Website. Each persona included key details such as goals, behaviors, frustrations, and background information.

Creating these personas helped shift the focus from designing based on assumptions to designing based on user needs.

Using these personas, we then developed user stories to better understand how different users would interact with the website. This helped identify potential usability issues and areas where the redesign could improve the overall experience.

docs.title: Applying This to the Redesign
docs.text:
These user focused design methods will directly influence future design decisions. By understanding user goals and pain points, the redesign can better support tasks such as browsing products, finding events, and navigating the site efficiently.

This ensures that design improvements are intentional and based on real user needs rather than personal preference.

docs.title: Importance in UX Design
docs.text:
User focused design is a core principle in UX/UI design and is widely used in our industry. Creating personas, user stories, and scenarios helps us as developers make design decisions that are based on real user needs rather than assumptions.

These methods are important not only for this project, but also for developing effective and user-focused products in real-world applications.

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
This week showed how important designing real user needs rather than assumptions. Creating personas and user stories helped understand user goals, behaviors, and frustrations.

This approach ensures that design decisions are intentional and grounded in how users actually interact with the website.

docs.title: Comms / UX / UI Design Redesign Project - Class 4
docs.text:
This week focused on creating clear problem statements to guide the redesign process. We learned that creating effective problem statements requires us to understand the ideal users and clearly define the problem and the purpose of the redesign.

We also learned a structure approach called 5 W's which help ensure the problem is well scoped and actionable.

We also discussed Information Architecture which focuses on organizing and categorizing items in the menu

docs.title: Problem Statement
docs.text:
A strong problem statement clearly defines the issue that the redesigned website aims to solve. It should be concise, focused, and based on user needs rather than assumptions.

Creating effective problem statements helps guide design decisions and ensures the redesign stays aligned with both user and business goals.

docs.title: The 5 W's
docs.text:
• Who — Who is using the site?

  • What — What is the site offering, and what should be the main focus?

  • Where — Where will the experience occur (primarily online)?

  • When — When will users need this solution (immediate vs. future use)?

  • Why — Why is the redesign necessary?

This helps ensure problems are clearly scoped and easier to tackle during the design process.

docs.title: Feature List
docs.text: The feature list defines the project scope and outlines what will be implemented in the website redesign. Having scope helps keep the project focused on user needs and business goals.

docs.title: Example Features
docs.text:
• Update branding (colors and typography)
  • Redesign home page layout
  • Improve navigation menu

docs.title: Ideation
docs.text:
This phase focuses on creating as many potential solutions as possible in a safe non-judgmental environment.

A class activity we did to accomplish this was Crazy 8's. We were tasked with rapid fire wireframe sketching, where we only had 30 seconds to sketch certain interfaces. The goal was to encourage creative thinking.

docs.title: Information Architecture
docs.text:
Information architecture (IA) focuses on organizing and labeling content so users can easily find what they need.

IF is comprised of several interconnected systems

  • Navigation design - Determines menu type, hierachy, and structure

    • Hierarchy
      • Deep - Few top-level links with many nested pages
      • Shallow - Man y top-level links with many nested pages
    • Labeling - Defines the language style used in menus

  • Content inventory - A complete list of site pages and content

  • Sitemap - A visual represetation of site structure
  • Wireframes - Simple layout sketches showing page structure

docs.title: Group Exercise
docs.text:
As a group, we practiced information architecture by categorizing the following items into approprite top-level menu headings:

Items included:

  • Accessibility
  • Audition Info
  • Contact Us
  • Current Shows
  • Donate
  • Getting Here
  • Join Our Team
  • Musical Theatre Camp
  • Our Story
  • Past Shows
  • Ticket Information
  • Venue Information
  • Visitor Questions (FAQ)
  • Volunteering

This activity demonstrated how proper organization improves clarity and usability. Well structured information architecture allows users to navigate more efficiently and supports the user experience.

It also connects to business goals, as clear navigation can improve user retention and engagement.

docs.title: Key insight
docs.text: This week highlighted the imporatnce of clearly defining our problems before designing solutions.

docs.title: Comms / UX / UI Design Redesign Project - Class 5
docs.text:
This week focused on understanding and creating user flows to support the redesign process.

User flows are diagrams that outline the steps a user takes to achieve a specific goal within a website or application.

They help visualize how users interact with a system and allow designers to identify potential problems in the experience.

docs.title: Example User Story
docs.text:
User flows are often bason on user stories.

Example: As a collector, I want to see the latest deals so that I can purchase items before they sell out.

docs.title: Example User Flow
docs.text:
Based on this user Story, we were able to create a user flow.

Start -> Open Menu -> Clicks "Latest Deals" -> View Products -> Select Product -> Add to card -> Enter payment information -> Complete purchase

This flow represents a typical path a user might take when interacting with a website.

docs.title: Application to the Redesign
docs.text:
Creating user flows helped the team understand how users move through the Magic Stronghold website and where improvements can be made.

It also ensured that important actions, such as browsing products or searching for events, are simple and user friendly.

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
This week showed that user flows are essential for understanding how users actually move through a website. Mapping out each step helped identify potential problems and ensure that important actions are clear.

Designing with user flows keeps the experience focused on user goals rather than assumptions.

docs.title: Comms / UX / UI Design Redesign Project - Class 6
docs.text: This week focused on preparing for user testing and learning the fundamentals of color and typography for our high-fidelity wireframes.

docs.title: User Testing Preparation
docs.text: User testing is an important step in deciding design decisions. It allows designers to observe how real users interact with wireframes and identify usability issues.

docs.title: Key Guidelines
docs.text:
• Wireframes should reflect the final design
  • Should include:
    • Clear headings and content for context
    • Functional links or navigation indicators
  • During testing:
    • Designers cannot guide or help the user
    • Only observe user behavior and take notes
    • Intervene only if something is clearly broken
  • In group settings:
    • One person acts as the facilitator(handles navigation)
    • One person acts as the observer(records user behavior)

This process helps find unexpected user actions and scenarios where the design may be confusing.

docs.title: High-Fidelity Design Preparation
docs.text:
We will first develop low-fidelity wireframes, working our way into developing our high-fidelity wireframe designs.

High-fidelity designs include:

  • Typography(fonts)
  • Color schemes
  • Visual styling(layout, spacing, images)

docs.title: Color Theory
docs.text:
Understanding color is important for creating visually effective and user friendly designs.

Additive vs Subtractive

  • Additive color
    • Starts with black (screen-based)
    • Uses Red, Green, and Blue (RGB)
    • Colors are created by adding light
  • Subtractive color
    • Starts with white(paper)
    • Colors are created by removing light
    • Common in print

docs.title: Color Requirements
docs.text:
For the high-fidelity design, the following requirements must be met:

  • Use a Three-Color scheme:
    • Primary color (most used)
    • Secondary color(supporting)
    • Accent color(used for emphasis)
  • Color scheme:
    • Cannot be only black and white
    • Must follow a valid color harmony
    • Cannot be analogoes or monochromatic
  • Accessibility:
    • Must meet at least WCAG AA contrast standards
    • Use tools like WebAIM Contrast Checker to validate color choices.

docs.title: Color and Branding
docs.text:
Color should be chosen to intentionally match the target audience and communicate the right message.

Different colors can influence how users perceive a product or brand, so color selection is a very important role in the overall user experience.

docs.title: Typography
docs.text: Typography helps improve readability and helps with visual clarity and hierarchy.

docs.title: Key considerations
docs.text:
• Select two fonts:
    • One for headings
    • One for body text
  • Fonts should:
    • Be readable and consistent
    • Match the tone of the website
    • Support clear hierarchy

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
This week showed that design is not only about layout, but also about testing and visual decisions.

Both user feedback and visual design elements are essential in creating an effective user experience.

docs.title: Comms / UX / UI Design Redesign Project - Class 7
docs.text: This week we focused on applying usability heuristics, defining our visual design choices, and refining the overall direction we are taking in our redesign.

docs.title: Usability Heuristics Applied
docs.text:
We selected and focused on three key heuristics, that will shape and guide our redesign.

1. Aesthetics and Minimalist Design

The original website felt cluttered and lacked clear visual hierarchy, making it difficult for users to focus on their intended purpose.

To tackle this problem the redesign will simplify the layouy by removing unnecessary elements and organizing content into sections. We will also try to increase whitespace, alignment issues, consistent image sizing, and a more fitting color scheme.

These changes will hopefully bring in a more user-friendly experience.

2. Consistency and Standards

The original website had inconsistent layouts, navigation, and visual styles, which made the site feel disconnected.

In the redesign we will improve the navigation, button styles, and layout structure to improve the consistency and overall standardizing the application to meet our usability heuristics.

This will hopefully let users navigate the website more easily, without having to learn different user flows across differnt pages

3. Match Between System and the Real World

The original website did not meet user expectations on how a card shop should behave and how it should be organized.

The redesign structures the website similarly to how a physical store organizes products, creating familiar sections. We also want to improve the event section and highlight important details such as the event type, dates, formats, and registration, which has been highly lacking in the original design.

This will create a more intuitive browsing experience that might be able to match user expectaions.

docs.title: Color Palette
docs.text:
• A triadic color scheme was chosen to provide visual balance while maintaining strong contrast for key elements

Primary Color (Most Used)

RGB(11, 46, 162) A deep blue used for the main background, reflecting the gaming and card shop aesthetic while maintaining consistency with the original branding.

Secondary Color (Supporting)

RGB(59, 85, 225) A lighter blue used for sections such as cards and categories to create hierarchy and visual separation.

Highlight Color (Accent)

RGB(255, 155, 39) An orange accent used for buttons, links, and key actions such as login, checkout, and event registration. This color provides strong contrast and draws user attention.

All color combinations were chosen to meet accessibility standards. Interactive elements will also include hover and active states to provide feedback beyond color alone.

docs.title: Typography
docs.text:
Typography was selected to improve readability and maintain a modern visual style, while keeping the appearance in mind as well.

Display/Heading Font

Montserrat or Roboto Used for headings and section titles due to their strong, modern appearance.

Body Font

Open Sans Used for body text because of its clarity and high readability.

These fonts are a strong standard pair for creating a clear visual hierarchy while maintaining consistency across the website.

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
This week showed how usability heuristics can influence design decisions. By applying principles like minimalism, consistency, and real-world alignment, the redesign became more intentional rather than based on assumptions.

Choosing colors and typography also reinforced that visual design is not just about appearance, but about improving usability, readability, and overall user experience.

docs.title: Comms / UX / UI Design Redesign Project - Class 8
docs.text:
This week focuses on live user testing using our low-fidelity wireframes. The goal was to observe how users interact with the design and identify usability issues without providing guidance.

These user scenarios are crucial for when we attempt to create our high-fidelity wireframes.

Three users participated in the testing process, each completing two main tasks related to product interaction and event registration.

docs.title: Main User Scenario/Flow Tasks
docs.text:
User Scenario 1: Subscribing to a product and recieving updates.

User optionally logs in -> User chooses product in navigation -> Selects product of choice -> Clicks subscribe to updates -> Log in if needed -> Confirmation window

User Scenario 2: Registering for a in-person event.

User optionally logs in -> User clicks on events -> Selects event of choice -> clicks on more details -> register/book event -> Log in if needed -> Confirmation window

docs.title: Observations
docs.text:
User 1 - Sam

Task 1 (Product / Subscription): Sam navigates to the product section but the product that they intended to visit was not there. This shows confusion about the structure of the product page

When attempting to subscribe, Sam was not confident when they were trying to subcribe, telling us that the context and/or labeling was unclear.

Task 2 (Events): Sam succesffuly navigated through the events section and completed the task without issues.

However, after completeing the process, there was no confirmation or success message, which created uncertainty about whether their action was completed.

User 2 - Ria

Task 1 (Product / Subscription): Ria navigated to the products section and seleceted a product correctly.

She then attempted to subscribe to updates, but there was no indication of whether she was logged in. This created confusion and told us that we need a confirmation window to show feedback to the user.

Also, after subscribing, the system redirected back to the homepage without confirmation, making the action feel incomplete.

Task 2 (Events): Ria completed the event registration process successfully.

Similar to User 1, the same issue occurred, no confirmation message after completing the action.

User 3 - Shervin

Task 1 (Product / Subscription): Shervin initially clicked the hero image, which redirected directly to a product page. This behavior showed that users may interpret large visual elements as primary navigation.

He then was able to successfully complete the subscription process after logging in.

Task 2 (Events): Shervin successfully navigated the events section, accessed event information, and completed the registration form without issues.

Sherving also stated that there was no confirmation window after booking an event.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
From the testing sessions, several problems occurred.

  • Users were confused about the product structure
  • No clear feedback after completing actions
  • Users expected confirmation messages or success pages
  • Navigation to events were clear and intuitive
  • Large visual elements can influence user behavior

docs.title: Design Improvements
docs.text: Based on these findings, the following improvements were identified: - Add clear leables and context for product subscription features - Implement confirmation messages or success pages after actions - Provide authentication feedback(prompt login) - Ensure consistent user flow after completing actions(avoid redirecting without feedback) - Review the role of the hero images

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
User testing revealed that even small gaps in feedback and clarity can significantly impact the user experience. While navigation was generally successful, missing confirmations and unclear context created confusion.

Addressing these issues will improve usability and make the redesign more intuitive giving us valuable feedback for our high-fidelity wireframes.

docs.title: Comms / UX / UI Design Redesign Project - Class 9
docs.text:
High-Fidelity Wireframes

This week focused on developing high-fidelity wireframes by applying finalized color schemes, typography, and visual styling to the redesign.

This week we also did in-person user testing and a review with Chris, which helped us get direct feedback and see what we needed to improve.

These designs build on previous wireframes and incorporate improvements based on user testing results.

docs.title: Strengths Identified
docs.text:
The high-fidelity wireframes showed many strong improvements:

  • Clear and consistent layout structure
  • Effective use of whitespace to improve readability
  • Intuitive navigation that supports user flow
  • Strong color choices that align with branding and accessibility
  • Well chosen typography that enhances visual hierarchy

Additionally, confirmation windows were significantly improved from earlier iterations, providing clearer feedback and better communication to users.

docs.title: Areas for Improvement
docs.text:
Based on feedback, several areas of improvements were identified:

Alignment: Elements such as buttons and content blocks need to be consistenly aligned to create a more finished and professional layout.

Spacing Consistency: Buttons and components should maintain equal spacing on all sides to improve visual balance.

Button Grouping: Related buttons should be placed closer together to reflect their relationship and improve usability.

Button Consistency: Buttons should follow consistent styles in terms of size, spacing, design, and color.

Confirmation Windows Design: Confirmation windows should be slightly smaller to improve whitespace and reduce visual clutter.

docs.title: Design Improvments
docs.text:
To improve and fix these issues our team will try to apply these changes:

  • Standardize alignment across all elements using consistent layout grids
  • Apply uniform spacing between components, sections, images
  • Group related actions together or simply try to make them closer to improve usability
  • Ensure all buttons follow a consistent design structure
  • Finishing touches on confirmation windows, white space and balance.

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
This week showed that even when the overall design is strong, small details such as alignment, spacing, and consistency have a big impact on the final user experience. Feedback from in-person testing and review showed areas that were not immediately obvious during design.

By fixing these details, the redesign becomes more polished, intuitive, and visually consistent, showing us the importance of having good structure in the process.
